title: Challenging Google Analytics: Building a Scalable, Cost-Effective User Tracking Service
gist: Delivery Hero's Alina Krasavina describes deprecating Google Analytics for an internal 'Perseus' tracking service that, years post-rollout, captures ~97% of data (vs GA's 85%), runs 3x cheaper, handles 10x the load, and gives GDPR control, real-time data, and unlimited event types Google couldn't provide.

insights.claim: Delivery Hero abandoned Google Analytics because a migration was required anyway (Universal Analytics deprecation), GA could not deliver the real-time data needed for billable advertisement use cases (it provided data only once or twice per day), and GA imposed a hard limit on the number of definable event types.
insights.speaker: Alina Krasavina
insights.so_what: Real-time and event-cardinality limits push large data-intensive firms off GA; signals churn risk in Google's analytics franchise among enterprises with billable telemetry.
insights.quote: it was providing the data once or twice per day, and we needed it just real-time

insights.claim: GDPR drove the migration because Google is a third party and Delivery Hero must store certain data in its own infrastructure for legal reasons, removing third-party data-transfer risk.
insights.speaker: Alina Krasavina
insights.so_what: Data-residency/compliance is a recurring forcing function for in-housing analytics in the EU — a tailwind for self-hosted and EU-sovereign telemetry stacks.
insights.quote: Google is a third party, and we cannot store all the data in the third party

insights.claim: After replacing Google Analytics, Delivery Hero improved its proprietary order-match-rate data-quality metric from GA's 85% to roughly 97% of data captured, which at scale on billable data represents significant revenue.
insights.speaker: Alina Krasavina
insights.so_what: Demonstrates an in-house build can beat Google on data completeness — a concrete counter to the assumption that hyperscaler tooling is always superior.
insights.quote: on Google Analytics, we had 85% of data receiving, and now we have roughly 97% of data

insights.claim: Delivery Hero's internal tracking service became three times cheaper than Google Analytics (improving from an initial 25% cost reduction) while handling 10x the load it tested against GA, using a deliberately simplistic, highly scalable architecture (API plus Pub/Sub processors writing to BigQuery).
insights.speaker: Alina Krasavina
insights.so_what: A 3x cost advantage at 10x scale validates the build-over-buy economics for high-volume telemetry; relevant to GA/SaaS-analytics pricing pressure.
insights.quote: it's three times cheaper than Google
insights.locator: A couple of years after that, we have the following results

insights.claim: Code-generated event models stored in third-party event schemas — surfacing data-type errors at application compile time — worked far better at improving producer data quality than showing metrics or asking developers to send accurate values, because the human factor defeats persuasion-based governance.
insights.speaker: Alina Krasavina
insights.so_what: Shift-left data-quality enforcement via compile-time schema checks is a reusable governance pattern that reduces downstream normalization cost.
insights.quote: If you have an error on compilation time, that works much better.
insights.locator: we introduced code generated event models

insights.claim: Delivery Hero solved data loss on pod restarts (OOM-killed pods losing buffered data) by making every request synchronous, accepting ~7x higher latency as acceptable because the SDK works non-blocking on the mobile app, and added gRPC writing directly to BigQuery as a doubled flow.
insights.speaker: Alina Krasavina
insights.so_what: Concrete reliability tradeoff (latency for zero data loss) instructive for billable-event pipelines where lost data equals lost revenue.
insights.quote: We resolved it with just making every request synchronous. It had increased latency like seven times maybe, but that was considered acceptable

insights.claim: Cost was cut through unglamorous storage tactics: archiving infrequently accessed data to cheaper storage, switching JSON stored as text to native JSON storage (lowering cost 20%), and using cheaper on-demand nodes instead of standard nodes.
insights.speaker: Alina Krasavina
insights.so_what: Storage-format and tiering optimizations deliver outsized savings in data-intensive systems — a FinOps lever for high-volume analytics.
insights.quote: we then just started storing it as a JSON, and it lowered the cost 20%
insights.locator: Cost improvement, that is, I think, the most obvious one

insights.claim: Delivery Hero is reusing the same tracking platform for additional use cases — feeding marketing campaigns on Facebook, storing application metrics data like Firebase, and experimentation data — turning a GA replacement into a general telemetry backbone.
insights.speaker: Alina Krasavina
insights.so_what: An in-house platform's expanding surface area increases switching costs away from external vendors and compounds the build's ROI.
